text:
I sat there and said, "I will go home and finish the job. Nobody wants me, and nobody… My life is a misery, so what's the use of me living?" Sitting out there in that field that night. God's grace. Wished I had more time. I can't … to get into that right there, to say what taken place, but someday I will, with God's help.
You might wonder how I got married. I finally found a girl that didn't drink and smoke. Just look around, they're still here. The backbone of the nation. So, she was a lovely girl. How glad… I'm glad to talk about her and her boy, and about her and I sitting back there listening at me. She was a queen. She was everything that a lady could be. I went with her. She was out of a good family. My family wasn't much. But, she was such a real nice girl, Christian girl. I'd go with her to church, that's where she'd taken me, to church.
text_no:
Jeg satt der og sa: "Jeg vil reise hjem og fullføre jobben. Ingen vil ha meg, og ingen… Livet mitt er en elendighet, så hva er poenget med å leve?" Jeg satt ute på marken den natten, Guds nåde. Jeg skulle ønske jeg hadde mer tid. Jeg kan ikke gå inn på detaljene akkurat nå, men en dag vil jeg det, med Guds hjelp.
Du lurer kanskje på hvordan jeg ble gift. Til slutt fant jeg en jente som verken drakk eller røykte. Se deg rundt, slike jenter finnes fortsatt. De er ryggraden i nasjonen. Hun var en vidunderlig jente. Jeg er så glad for å kunne snakke om henne og sønnen hennes, og om oss der vi satt og lyttet på meg. Hun var en dronning. Hun var alt en dame kunne være. Jeg var sammen med henne. Hun kom fra en god familie. Min familie var ikke mye verdt. Men hun var en virkelig flott jente, en kristen jente. Jeg fulgte henne til menigheten; det var hun som tok meg med dit.

text: And I remember my conversion, when I was converted. I will just have to bypass most of it to hurry up, now. And I remember in the … I begin to make up my mind that she was too good of a girl for me just to go with her. Somebody ought to marry her who would make her a good living, and I was only making twenty cents an hour, so I knowed I couldn't make her no living at that, working as a ditch digger. And her father made five hundred and something a month during the time of the depression, a brotherhood organizer on the Pennsylvania Railroad. But, she loved me, and I loved her. So, I thought there was just going to be a … I'd just have to tell her and just walk away. And I couldn't do it. I tried to. Every night I thought I'd tell her I just wouldn't come back no more, and let her go ahead and get with some good boy that would … could make her a living, and make her happy. And I was taking up her time, her young life, and I didn't want to do that. So, I was all mixed up. And I didn't want to give her up because I loved her too much. And I certainly was in a bad shape.
text_no: Jeg husker min omvendelse, og jeg vil raskt gå gjennom den. Jeg husker at jeg begynte å innse at hun var for god til bare å date. Noen burde gifte seg med henne og gi henne et godt liv, og jeg tjente bare tjue cent i timen som grøftegraver. Hennes far tjente over fem hundre dollar i måneden under depresjonen som broderskapsorganisator på Pennsylvania Railroad. Men vi elsket hverandre. Jeg tenkte at jeg måtte fortelle henne at det var på tide å gå videre og finne en god gutt som kunne forsørge henne og gjøre henne lykkelig. Jeg slet hver natt med å fortelle henne at jeg ikke ville komme tilbake, slik at hun kunne finne noen bedre. Jeg ønsket ikke å kaste bort hennes tid og unge liv. Jeg var helt forvirret, for jeg ville ikke gi henne opp fordi jeg elsket henne så mye. Jeg var virkelig i en vanskelig situasjon.

text:
So, I was too bashful to ask her to marry me. I just couldn't do that. Guess you wonder how I ever got married. I wrote her a letter and asked her, if you would. It worked. Now, it wasn't, "Dear Miss," it was, you know, a little more than that. I sat down one day and fixed it all up, and fixed me up a letter.
Now, her father was just a good friend. Her mother was a good woman, but she was out of a real starchy church, you know, that she believed in. Guys like myself, I guess, wasn't too much. So, I thought, "I could get by with her daddy, but her mother is what was bothering me."
text_no:
Jeg var for sky til å spørre henne om å gifte seg med meg. Jeg klarte bare ikke å gjøre det. Du lurer sikkert på hvordan jeg i det hele tatt ble gift. Jeg skrev et brev til henne og spurte, og det fungerte. Det var ikke et formelt "Kjære frøken"-brev, men noe mer personlig. En dag satte jeg meg ned, fikk alt på plass og skrev brevet.
Hennes far var en god venn, og hennes mor var en god kvinne, men hun tilhørte en streng menighet som hun trodde sterkt på. Fyrer som meg selv ble kanskje ikke vurdert så høyt. Jeg tenkte: "Jeg kan få godkjennelse fra faren, men det er moren som bekymrer meg."

text: So, I just went to work that morning. I said, "Now, if it don't work, all right, so that will settle it." And I closed the letter and dropped it in the mailbox, and went to work. And I had to take her to church on Wednesday night. Had a date. So I went over and put it in the box Monday morning. So, Wednesday night come along, I had to go to church. And I happened to get to thinking, you know. Never thought of it before, but me mailing that letter, what if her mammy got a hold of it, and she didn't get it? I thought, "Oh, my. I'd sure be in for it when I get up there, if her mother got it besides, she didn't get it." Well, the more I thought of it, the more I thought I'd better stay away Wednesday night.
text_no: Samme morgen dro jeg på jobb. Jeg sa til meg selv: "Hvis dette ikke fungerer, er det greit, så får vi avgjøre det da." Så jeg avsluttet brevet, la det i postkassen og dro på jobb. Jeg skulle ta henne med til menigheten onsdag kveld. Vi hadde en avtale. Jeg la brevet i postkassen mandag morgen. Da onsdag kveld kom, måtte jeg dra til menigheten. Da begynte jeg å tenke. Jeg hadde ikke tenkt på det før, men hva om moren hennes fikk tak i brevet og hun ikke fikk det? Jeg tenkte: "Å, nei. Jeg kommer til å få problemer hvis moren hennes får det og ikke hun." Jo mer jeg tenkte på det, jo mer innså jeg at jeg burde holde meg unna onsdag kveld.

text:
"Well," I thought, "no, I can't do that, now. I've got to go. So, what am I going to do about it?" So, I thought, "I'll just drive up out front, and go easy." I knowed better than to drive up out front and blow the horn. And boys, I'm telling you that now, too, and to you girls, if your boyfriend don't think enough of you to come up to the house, ask for you, keep away from him. That's right.
So, I pulled the old Ford up out there, it was stopped. Got up on the porch, I thought, "I just won't go in the house, you know. She'd get me in the house, then I'd be in an awful fix." So, I knocked at the door. And so, Hope, my wife, or sweetheart then, she come to the door. She said, "Hello, Billy. Come on in."
text_no:
"Vel," tenkte jeg, "nei, det kan jeg ikke gjøre nå. Jeg må dra. Så hva skal jeg gjøre med det?" Jeg tenkte, "Jeg kjører bare opp foran, og tar det rolig." Jeg visste bedre enn å kjøre opp foran og tute med hornet. Og gutter, jeg sier dette til dere nå, og til dere jenter også: Hvis kjæresten deres ikke bryr seg nok om dere til å komme til huset og be om dere, hold dere unna ham. Det er riktig.
Så jeg parkerte den gamle Ford-en utenfor. Jeg kom opp på verandaen og tenkte: "Jeg går bare ikke inn i huset. Hvis hun får meg inn, blir jeg sittende fast." Jeg banket på døren. Hope, min daværende kjæreste og nå kone, kom til døren. Hun sa: "Hei, Billy. Kom inn."

text:
And I thought, "Uh-oh." "No, no. I'm afraid to come in. If your mother got that letter, then I'm going to get me in the house I can't get out, then. So, I would be in an awful fix."
So, she said, "Won't you step in?"
I said, "Thank you," I said, I'll just wait right here on the porch."
And she said, "Oh, come on in."
My, I walked in sit down at the door, and hold my hat in my hand. I thought, "Oh, this is sure under a lot of strain."
After a while, her mother come in. She said, "How do you do, William?"
I said, "How do, Mrs. Brumbach? Sure a fine day."
"Yes, sure."
So, went on. I thought, "She never got that letter."
text_no:
Jeg tenkte: «Å nei. Jeg tør ikke gå inn. Hvis moren din har fått det brevet, vil jeg sitte fast i huset og ikke komme meg ut igjen. Da vil jeg være i en forferdelig situasjon.»
Hun sa, «Vil du ikke komme inn?»
Jeg svarte, «Tusen takk, men jeg venter heller her på verandaen.»
Hun insisterte, «Å, kom nå inn.»
Jeg gikk inn og satte meg ned like ved døren, holdt hatten i hånden og tenkte: «Dette er virkelig stressende.»
Etter en stund kom moren hennes inn og sa: «Hvordan har du det, William?»
Jeg svarte, «God dag, fru Brumbach. Det er en nydelig dag.»
«Ja, absolutt.»
Samtalen fortsatte, og jeg tenkte: «Hun har aldri fått det brevet.»

text:
Went on to church. I didn't hear a thing Dr. Davis said that night. Only thing I was thinking about, "Just as soon as church is over, she's going to tell me, 'All right, this is your last night.' I was going to lose my girl, then." Had that on my mind. You know how the Devil can tell you lies. So, then I thought, "I will lose my girl as soon as church is over."
So then, I never heard nothing the preacher said. When we went home that night, she said, "Let's just walk."
text_no:
Jeg gikk videre til menigheten. Jeg hørte ikke et ord av hva Dr. Davis sa den kvelden. Det eneste jeg tenkte på var: "Så snart møtet er over, kommer hun til å si, 'Greit, dette er din siste natt.'" Jeg tenkte at jeg kom til å miste jenta mi. Den tanken dominerte hodet mitt. Du vet hvordan Djevelen kan fylle hodet med løgner. Jeg var overbevist om at jeg ville miste henne så snart møtet var over.
Derfor hørte jeg ingenting av hva forkynneren sa. Da vi kom hjem den kvelden, sa hun: "La oss bare gå en tur."

text:
I thought, "Uh-oh. I know she got the letter, now." So, I was just walking on down the street. I kept looking over at her. Oh, my, those dark eyes, and that moon shining down, you know. I just hated to have to lose her. So, I thought, "I know I can't marry a girl like that. So, I guess maybe I just go ahead and have to be an old hermit." Always said I was going to have a bunch of traps and a dog, and live back in the woods. So, thought, "Well now, I just guess this is it."
And we kept on walking, and got almost to the house, why, I thought, "You know, maybe she didn't get that letter. Maybe it just hung up in the mailbox, and she didn't get it." So, I got pretty bold, you know, then. I was talking right along. She didn't get the mail, so I was kind of glad she didn't.
text_no:
Jeg tenkte, "Å nei. Jeg vet hun fikk brevet nå." Så jeg gikk nedover gaten. Jeg kikket stadig bort på henne. Åh, de mørke øynene og måneskinnet. Jeg hatet tanken på å miste henne. Jeg tenkte, "Jeg kan ikke gifte meg med en jente som henne. Jeg antar jeg må bli en gammel eneboer." Jeg hadde alltid sagt at jeg skulle ha mange feller og en hund, og bo i skogen. Så jeg tenkte, "Vel, dette er vel min skjebne."
Vi fortsatte å gå og nærmet oss huset. Jeg tenkte, "Kanskje hun ikke fikk det brevet. Kanskje det ble hengende i postkassen, og hun ikke har sett det." Jeg ble ganske modig da. Jeg snakket i vei. Hun hadde ikke hentet posten, så jeg var litt glad for det.

text:
So, then I was going along, you know, talking fine. Just about the time we got almost to the house, she said, "Billy?"
And I said, "Yes, Hope?"
And she said, "I got your letter."
Oh, my. I said, "You did?"
She said, "Uh-huh."
Well, I thought, "This is it."
So, walked on a little farther, and she just walked on just as quietly. You know how a woman can keep you under suspense, you know. I thought, "Well, say something. Tell me not to come back no more, or do something." So, I just kept walking on. I looked over at her, and she looked over at me, and just kept on going. So, I thought, "Well, what are you going to say?" Well, she just kept on walking. Just never said nothing. I thought I'd kind of break the ice, and I said, "Did you read it?"
She said, "Uh huh."
text_no:
Så, da jeg gikk der og snakket som vanlig, nesten til vi var ved huset, sa hun, "Billy?"
Jeg svarte, "Ja, Hope?"
Hun sa, "Jeg fikk brevet ditt."
Å, kjære. Jeg svarte, "Gjorde du det?"
Hun sa, "Uh-huh."
Jeg tenkte, "Nå skjer det."
Vi gikk litt lenger, og hun fortsatte å gå stille. Du vet hvordan en kvinne kan holde deg i spenning. Jeg tenkte, "Si noe. Fortell meg at jeg ikke skal komme tilbake mer, eller si noe i det hele tatt."
Jeg fortsatte å gå. Jeg så på henne, og hun så på meg, uten å si noe. Jeg tenkte, "Hva vil du si?" Men hun fortsatte bare å gå uten å si et ord. Jeg tenkte at jeg skulle bryte isen litt og spurte, "Leste du det?"
Hun svarte, "Uh-huh."

text:
Well, I thought, "Well, comment it someway." So, didn't say a word, just kept on going. We was just a little piece from the house, and I thought, "You're going to lead me right up to your mother, aren't you?" "And then I know they're going to have it."
And so I said, "Did you read it all?"
She said, "Uh huh."
That's all I could get out of her, "Uh huh."
So, I said, "What did you think about it?"
She said, "Oh, it was all right." We got married. It worked fine. Yes, sir.
The trouble of it was, we had to ask her parents. And she said, "Billy," said, "you will have to ask mother and dad."
text_no:
Jeg tenkte, "Kommenter det på en eller annen måte." Så jeg sa ingenting, bare fortsatte. Vi var bare et lite stykke fra huset, og jeg tenkte, "Du kommer til å lede meg rett til moren din, gjør du ikke?" "Og da vet jeg at de vil ha det."
Så jeg spurte, "Leste du hele greia?"
Hun svarte bare, "Uh huh."
Det var alt jeg fikk ut av henne, "Uh huh."
Jeg spurte, "Hva syntes du om det?"
Hun sa, "Å, det var greit." Vi giftet oss. Det fungerte bra. Ja, sir.
Problemet var at vi måtte spørre foreldrene hennes. Hun sa, "Billy, du må spørre mor og far."

text:
I said, "Look, Hope," I said, "you know, the best I remember, now, that married life is supposed to be fifty-fifty. See?" I said, "Now, I tell you what, I'll make an agreement with you." I said, "My fifty part, I will ask your father. And your fifty part, you ask your mother." I knew I could get by with her dad, but I was doubting her mother.
She said, "All right." She said, "If you will ask dad first."
I said, "Well, all right."
She said, "We ought to ask him right away."
I said, "I guess that's right."
text_no:
Jeg sa, "Hør her, Hope, så vidt jeg husker, skal ekteskapet være femti-femti, ikke sant?" Jeg fortsatte, "La oss gjøre en avtale. Jeg tar min del og spør faren din, og du tar din del og spør moren din." Jeg visste at jeg kunne få det til med hennes far, men jeg var usikker på moren hennes.
Hun sa, "Greit. Men du må spørre pappa først."
Jeg svarte, "OK."
Hun sa, "Vi burde spørre ham med en gang."
Jeg svarte, "Det er sant."

text:
So, I went up to her house, that night, and sat up there. And I got ready to go. We was all sitting, and Mr. Brumbach was setting over at his desk typing off something, you know. And oh, my. I just thought I'd stay all night. So, finally got time, I had to say something. So, I said … Got nine-thirty, that was my time to go home. How times have changed. So, I said … I got up to go home, and so I said, "Good night, you all."
Walked out, and Hope walked over and said, "Didn't you--didn't you ask? Why don't you ask him?"
I said, "Oh, I just can't." I said, "I just can't."
She said, "Well, you got to ask him."
And I said, "Well, you take your mother and go in the other room."
And she said, "All right."
So, she went back and said, "Mom, would you come out here a minute?"
So, I stood there at the door a little bit. And I said, "Charlie?"
text_no:
Så jeg dro opp til huset hennes den kvelden og satt der. Jeg gjorde meg klar til å dra, og vi satt alle sammen. Mr. Brumbach satt ved pulten sin og skrev noe, vet du. Og åh, jeg tenkte jeg kunne bli hele natten. Men til slutt kom tiden da jeg måtte si noe. Klokken ble halv ti, min tid til å dra hjem. Så jeg reiste meg og sa, "God natt, alle sammen."
Jeg gikk ut, og Hope kom bort og sa, "Spurte du ikke? Hvorfor spør du ham ikke?"
Jeg svarte, "Jeg kan bare ikke." Jeg sa, "Jeg bare kan ikke."
Hun sa, "Du må spørre ham."
Jeg sa, "Ta med deg moren din og gå inn i et annet rom."
Hun svarte, "All right."
Så gikk hun tilbake og sa, "Mamma, kan du komme hit et øyeblikk?"
Jeg stod der ved døren en stund og sa, "Charlie?"

text:
He was writing on something. He turned around and said, "Huh? What did you say, Bill?"
And I said, "Could I--could I speak to you a minute?"
He turned around and said, "Yes, why? What's the matter. Bill?"
And I said, "Would you come here just a minute?"
He said, "Yes."
He walked out on the porch. And oh, I was sweating, and my heart beating so fast. And I said, "Sure is a pretty night, isn't it, Charlie?"
He said, "Sure is, Bill."
I said, "I like these kind of nights."
He said, "You can have her." How he spared me!
I said, "You mean it?"
He said, "Yes."
I said, "What about her mother?"
He said, "I will take care of that."
text_no:
Han skrev på noe. Han snudde seg og sa: «Hæ? Hva sa du, Bill?»
Jeg svarte: «Kan jeg--kan jeg snakke med deg et øyeblikk?»
Han snudde seg og sa: «Ja, hvorfor det? Hva er i veien, Bill?»
Jeg sa: «Kan du komme hit et øyeblikk?»
Han sa: «Ja.»
Han kom ut på verandaen. Jeg svettet og hjertet banket raskt. Jeg sa: «Det er en vakker natt, ikke sant, Charlie?»
Han sa: «Det er det, Bill.»
Jeg sa: «Jeg liker slike netter.»
Han sa: «Du kan få henne.» Hvordan han skånet meg!
Jeg sa: «Mener du det?»
Han sa: «Ja.»
Jeg sa: «Hva med moren hennes?»
Han sa: «Jeg ordner det.»

text: I said, "Thank you, Charlie." I said, "Now look, Charlie," I said, "I know you can get her good clothes and everything. And there's probably many more boys around here who could make her a better living, and she's a pretty girl, and a nice lady," and I said, "anybody would want to go with her." I said, "I don't know just how breaks ever happen like that for me." I said, "Charlie, there's nobody in the world loves her any more than I do." I said, "I can't do for her like you can, because I only make about twenty cents an hour." I said, "Charlie, I'll work as hard as I can, and do everything that I know is in my power to make her a living and be good to her."
text_no: Jeg sa, "Takk, Charlie." Jeg fortsatte, "Hør nå, Charlie. Jeg vet du kan gi henne fine klær og alt mulig annet. Det er sikkert mange andre gutter her som kan gi henne et bedre liv. Hun er en pen jente og en hyggelig kvinne, og hvem som helst ville ønske å være sammen med henne." Jeg sa, "Jeg vet ikke hvordan slike ting skjer med meg." Jeg la til, "Charlie, det er ingen i verden som elsker henne mer enn jeg gjør. Jeg kan ikke gi henne det samme som du kan, fordi jeg tjener bare tjue cent i timen. Charlie, jeg skal jobbe så hardt jeg kan og gjøre alt som står i min makt for å forsørge henne og være god mot henne."

text:
I never will forget. And he laid his… He was a German, and me an Irishman, and we was always guying one another. Laid his big hand over on my shoulder, said, "Bill, I'd rather you'd have her than anybody I know of." He said, "Life don't mean what you've got. It's how contented you are with what you have."
I said, "Thank you, Charlie. I love her." And I said, "I'll be good to her, and true. And I'll work as hard as I can for her."
He said, "I believe that."
We got married. Moved into a little two-room place. I never forget what we bought to go housekeeping. And I will be done just in a minute.
text_no:
Jeg vil aldri glemme det. Han la hånden sin på skulderen min. Han var tysker, og jeg en ire, og vi ertet alltid hverandre. Han la den store hånden sin på skulderen min og sa: “Bill, jeg vil heller at du skal ha henne enn noen andre jeg kjenner.” Han sa: “Livet handler ikke om hva du har, men hvor fornøyd du er med det du har."
Jeg sa: “Takk, Charlie. Jeg elsker henne. Jeg skal være god mot henne og trofast. Og jeg skal jobbe så hardt jeg kan for henne."
Han sa: “Det tror jeg på."
Vi giftet oss og flyttet inn i en liten toroms leilighet. Jeg glemmer aldri hva vi kjøpte for å begynne husholdningen. Jeg skal snart være ferdig.

text: I went down, I had enough money to go to Sears and Roebucks and make a payment on a breakfast set that had … [Blank spot on tape] … Paint was yellow, and put a great big green shamrock right on every chair. An Irishman, you know. And we had an old folding bed. How many ever knowed what a folding bed is? Somebody give it to us. And I went over to a junk dealer and got a stove for seventy-five cents, a cooking stove. And I had to pay a dollar and something to get grates to go in it. And we put it in there and went to housekeeping. We didn't have very much, but we were happy. We had one another. That's all that mattered. We loved one another, and that settled it.
text_no: Jeg dro ned, og jeg hadde nok penger til å gå til Sears og Roebuck og betale på en frokostsetning som hadde … [Tomt.område.på.lydbånd] … Malingen var gul, og jeg satte en stor grønn kløver på hver stol. En irsk mann, vet du. Og vi hadde en gammel klappseng. Hvor mange vet hva en klappseng er? Noen ga den til oss. Jeg dro til en skraphandler og kjøpte en komfyr for syttifem cent, en kokeovn. Og jeg måtte betale litt over en dollar for å få rister til den. Vi satte den inn og startet husholdningen. Vi hadde ikke mye, men vi var lykkelige. Vi hadde hverandre. Det var alt som betydde noe. Vi elsket hverandre, og det var alt som trengtes.

text:
Later on along, God gave us a little boy, standing right back there looking at me now. And how happy we were when this little fellow come into the world. And we loved God with all of our heart.
Just before, or just right after the boy was born, I took my first vacation. We saved up enough money till I had, I believe, around six, eight, ten dollars saved up, besides the car payment. And I went up to Michigan to visit an old friend of mine that I had met, by the name of John Ryan, sitting right here now.
I didn't know too much about Pentecostal people. And this old man, I thought, belonged to the House of David, because he wore long hair and beard. But, I found out it was wrong. I went to visit him. And yes, you remember, Brother Ryan. We stayed up there a few days, and on my road back is when I got my first acquaintance with Pentecostal people. We come through Mishawaka, and there was a … Mishawaka, Indiana. And there was a great convention going on. And there were old Fords, and Cadillacs, and everything setting around there, and I heard a lot of noise. And I went in to listen to these people. And they were shouting, "Whooo," or they're dancing, and running, and screaming. I thought, "Tut, tut, tut, tut, such manners in the church." Ha. And how they were going on. I thought, "Well, that's terrible for people to act like that in church." So, I listened at them. They were running up and down the floor dancing, and screaming, and carrying on. I thought, "That's just awful that they'd do that." My self-styled, Baptist ways, you know, so I thought …
text_no:
Senere ga Gud oss en liten gutt, som står der borte og ser på meg nå. Vi var så glade da denne lille karen kom til verden, og vi elsket Gud av hele vårt hjerte.
Rett før eller rett etter gutten ble født, tok jeg min første ferie. Vi hadde spart opp nok penger, tror jeg hadde rundt seks, åtte, ti dollar ferdig spart, bortsett fra bilbetalingen. Jeg dro til Michigan for å besøke en gammel venn, John Ryan, som nå sitter her.
Jeg visste ikke så mye om pinsemenigheten. Denne gamle mannen, trodde jeg, tilhørte Davids hus fordi han hadde langt hår og skjegg. Men det viste seg å være feil. Jeg besøkte ham, og ja, du husker det, Bror Ryan. Vi ble der noen dager, og på vei tilbake stiftet jeg mitt første bekjentskap med pinsevenner. Vi kom gjennom Mishawaka, Indiana, hvor det pågikk et stort stevne. Der stod gamle Fords, Cadillacs og alt mulig parkert, og jeg hørte mye støy. Jeg gikk inn for å lytte til disse menneskene. De ropte, "Whooo," danset, løp og skrek. Jeg tenkte, "For en oppførsel i menigheten." Og hvordan de holdt på. Jeg syntes det var forferdelig at folk oppførte seg slik i Kirken. Så jeg lyttet til dem. De løp opp og ned gulvet, danset, skrek og bar seg. Jeg tenkte, "Det er bare forferdelig det de gjør." Med mine selvrettferdige, baptistiske måter, vet du, så tenkte jeg...

text:
So, that night I just waited to see what they would do. They had all the preachers come up on the platform.
They said, "We're had about five hundred of us up there, and he said, "Now, we haven't got time for you to say nothing, but just say who you are, and where you're from."
So, I just said, "William Branham, Jeffersonville." Went…
*
Next day, there was something or another, them preacher's preaching, they brought an old man out there, an old colored man, had a great big old long coat on. All them preachers had been preaching about different things that day, but he took his text from over in Job. "Where was you when I laid the foundations of the world…", and so forth. They had been preaching about earthly things, and he preached about heavenly things. He took Christ up before the foundation of the world, brought Him in the second coming, down the horizontal rainbow.
text_no:
Den kvelden ventet jeg bare for å se hva de ville gjøre. De hadde alle forkynnerne oppe på plattformen. 
De sa: "Vi er omtrent fem hundre her oppe, og vi har ikke tid til at dere sier mye, så bare presenter dere og si hvor dere er fra."
Jeg sa: "William Branham, Jeffersonville." Gikk bort...
Neste dag, mens noen prekede, førte de fram en gammel mann, en eldre mørkhudet mann med en stor, lang frakk. Alle forkynnerne hadde den dagen talt om ulike ting, men han tok sin tekst fra Job: "Hvor var du da Jeg la jordens grunnvoller?". Mens de andre talte om jordiske ting, talte han om himmelske ting. Han løftet Kristus opp før verdens grunnvoll ble lagt, og førte Ham til Hans andre komme, nedover regnbuen.

text:
When they brought the old fellow out there, he was so old they had to lead him out. And about time he got all wound up to preaching, he jumped up in the air, clicked his heels together, said, "Glory to God! Whew!" He said, "There's not enough room here for me to preach," and walked off the platform.
And I said, "Boy, if it will make an old man act like that, I want that too. What will it do for me?" I said, "That's what I want."
I didn't have any money, so I couldn't eat with them. I had seventy-five, or eighty cents left besides my gasoline I had to buy. I couldn't stay in a tourist court, so I went and bought me a big sack full of second day rolls, you know. So, I'd eaten them. I went out in an old cornfield and laid down that night, and I pressed my trousers between the two seats, you know, back seat and front seat, and put them in there and pressed it out. And I prayed all night for God to give me favor with those people. They had something that I wanted. And so I thought, "Oh, that's what I been looking for, right there."
text_no:
Da de førte ut den gamle mannen, var han så gammel at de måtte lede ham. Men da han endelig kom i gang med å forkynne, hoppet han opp i luften, klasket hælene sammen og ropte, "Ære være Gud! Puh!" Han sa, "Det er ikke nok plass her for meg å forkynne," og gikk av plattformen.
Jeg sa, "Hvis det kan få en gammel mann til å oppføre seg slik, vil jeg også ha det. Hva kan det gjøre for meg?" Jeg sa, "Det er det jeg vil ha."
Jeg hadde ikke penger, så jeg kunne ikke spise med dem. Jeg hadde syttifem eller åtti øre igjen etter å ha kjøpt bensin. Jeg kunne ikke bo på et turiststed, så jeg kjøpte en stor pose med gårsdagens rundstykker. Jeg spiste dem og la meg ned i en gammel maisåker den natten. Jeg presset buksene mellom de to setene, baksetet og forsetet, og ba hele natten om at Gud skulle gi meg gunst hos disse menneskene. De hadde noe jeg ønsket. Jeg tenkte, "Å, det er det jeg har lett etter."

text:
So, I went back down the next morning, and I'd all shined up, best I had, had a T-shirt on, and seersucker trousers. Nobody knowed me, anyhow. So, I went in and sat down. The colored folks was there. They had to have it above the Mason-Dixon Line so the colored could sit around. And I happened to sit down, and first thing you know a colored man sat down by me, and I'm a southerner too, you know. I looked around and thought, "Now, this ain't right." Looked at him. First thing you know, they come up. All that big group of people, all of them singing, and going on like that. And I thought, "This is wonderful."
So, the man come out. He said, "Last night on the platform, there was a young evangelist by the name of William Branham." Said, "Anybody know the whereabouts of him?" Two or three thousand people there. Seersucker trousers a T-shirt, so I just sat real close like this. Said, "Anybody knows where William Branham is? We want him to bring the morning message." Morning message, seersucker trousers, and T-shirt. I just hunkered down real easy like this, you know, down under the seat.
text_no:
Neste morgen dro jeg tilbake, grundig stelt og iført en T-skjorte og seersucker-bukser, det beste jeg hadde. Ingen kjente meg uansett. Jeg gikk inn og satte meg ned. De fargede folkene var til stede. De måtte ha det nord for Mason-Dixon linjen, slik at fargede kunne sitte rundt. Jeg satte meg, og før jeg visste ordet av det, kom en farget mann og satte seg ved siden av meg. Jeg er også sørstatsmann, vet du. Jeg så meg rundt og tenkte: "Dette er ikke riktig." Jeg så på ham. Men før jeg visste ordet av det, begynte alle de store gruppene av mennesker å synge. Jeg tenkte: "Dette er fantastisk."
Så kom mannen ut. Han sa: "I går kveld på plattformen var det en ung evangelist ved navn William Branham. Er det noen som vet hvor han er?" To eller tre tusen mennesker var der. Iført seersucker-bukser og en T-skjorte, satt jeg bare helt stille. Han sa: "Er det noen som vet hvor William Branham er? Vi vil at han skal bringe morgenens budskap." Jeg krøkte meg sammen under setet, iført mine seersucker-bukser og T-skjorte.

text:
So, he announced again. Said, "Anybody outside, if you know where William Branham is, tell him come in." Nobody knowed me.
So, this colored man looked around and said, "You know the man?"
I had to lie, or do something, you know. So, I said, "Look, I know him, yes."
He said, "Well, go get him."
I said, "Look, don't say nothing." I said, "I'm he, see."
He said, "Well, get up there."
And I said, "Why, I can't do it." I said, "Look here the way I'm dressed."
He said, "Them people don't care how you dress. Go on up there."
And I said, "No, think…" I said, "Shhh. Don't say nothing like that."
He said, "Anybody found William Branham?"
He said, "Here he is. Here he is." Oh, my. "Here he is."
text_no:
Så, han annonserte igjen og sa, "Er det noen utenfor som vet hvor William Branham er, få ham til å komme inn." Ingen kjente meg.
Da så denne fargede mannen rundt seg og spurte, "Kjenner du mannen?"
Jeg måtte lyve, eller gjøre noe, forstår du. Så jeg sa, "Jeg kjenner ham, ja."
Han sa, "Vel, gå og hent ham."
Jeg sa, "Ikke si noe." Jeg sa, "Jeg er ham, ser du."
Han sa, "Vel, gå opp dit."
Jeg sa, "Jeg kan ikke gjøre det." Jeg sa, "Se hvordan jeg er kledd."
Han sa, "De bryr seg ikke om hvordan du er kledd. Gå opp der."
Jeg sa, "Nei, tenk…" Jeg sa, "Shhh. Ikke si noe sånt."
Han sa, "Er William Branham funnet?"
Han sa, "Her er han. Her er han." Å, herlighet. "Her er han."

text:
Oh, my! Seersucker trousers with a T-shirt. Here I went up. I never seen a microphone before. And here I went walking up in this great big, cathedral of a place, there, walking up there, you know, and I thought, "Oh, how out of place."
I took my text of when the rich man, in Luke, you know, lifted up his eyes in hell, and he cried. I got up there and I said, "And he lifted up. There was no children in hell, so he cried. There was no flowers, so he cried. There was no prayer meeting, and he cried. And there's no this, that, and the other." I cried. And the first thing you know, the Holy Spirit got caught into that building, and I never seen such a carrying on in all… Why, I went unconscious nearly. I was right in the right place and didn't know it.
text_no:
Å, du store! Seersucker-bukser med en T-skjorte. Der gikk jeg opp. Jeg hadde aldri sett en mikrofon før. Og der gikk jeg, opp i dette enorme katedral-lignende stedet, og jeg følte meg så malplassert.
Jeg tok utgangspunkt i historien om den rike mannen i Lukas, du vet, der han løfter opp sine øyne i helvete og gråter. Jeg kom opp der og sa: "Og han løftet opp. Det var ingen barn i helvete, så han gråt. Det var ingen blomster, så han gråt. Det var ingen bønnemøter, og han gråt. Og det var ingen dette, det og det andre." Jeg gråt. Og plutselig, før jeg visste ordet av det, fylte Den Hellige Ånd bygningen, og jeg hadde aldri sett noe lignende. Jeg ble nesten bevisstløs. Jeg var virkelig på rett sted uten å vite det.

text:
After I got outside, we come to. I got outside, and there was a fellow walked up to me with a great big Texas hat on, a pair of cowboy boats. Said, "Say, I'm Reverend So-and-so."
I said, "Well, say. Maybe my seersucker trousers is not so bad."
Another fellow walked up and had on these little old golf plain clothes, you know. Said, "I'm Doctor So-and-so from down in Florida." He said, "Will you come preach for me?"
Well, I thought, "My!"
I had a whole string of invitations. And I got in my old Ford, I was going to tell my wife. And down the road I went. She made thirty miles an hour. That's fifteen miles this way, and fifteen miles up and down that way, you know. Down the road I come, just as hard as I could go. I'd pull on the old brake out there, and the two back wheels scooted. Bless her heart, she run to the door, and her arms open, you know, and she said, "Did you have a good time?"
text_no:
Etter at jeg kom ut, møtte vi hverandre. Ute møtte jeg en fyr med en stor Texas-hatt og cowboyboots. Han sa: "Hei, jeg er Pastor Så-og-så."
Jeg svarte: "Vel, kanskje mine seersucker-bukser ikke er så ille."
En annen kar kom bort i enkle golfklær. Han sa: "Jeg er Doktor Så-og-så fra Florida. Vil du komme og forkynne for meg?"
Jeg tenkte: "Jøss!"
Jeg fikk en hel rekke invitasjoner. Jeg satte meg i min gamle Ford for å fortelle kona. Jeg kjørte avgårde. Hun gjorde tretti mil i timen – femten mil fremover og femten mil opp og ned, vet du. Jeg kjørte så raskt jeg kunne og dro i den gamle bremsen. De to bakhjulene skled. Kona løp til døren med åpne armer og spurte: "Hadde du det bra?"

text:
I said, "Oh, a wonderful time!" Telling her about being with Brother Ryan here, and so forth. So I said, "Honey, I got something to tell you. Just let me show you." Reached down in my pocket. "See all them?" I said, "I've always wanted to be an evangelist." I said, "There is … I've got enough invitations to last me all year. You go with me?"
Said, "Sure."
Well, we still owed about a hundred dollars on the old Ford, and debts and things, but she wanted to go with me.
Well, we went and told her mother. "Mom," she said, "go ahead." But her mother said, "Bill, no." She said, "That ain't nothing but a bunch of backwash out of other churches. Just what other churches had kicked out."
text_no:
Jeg sa, "Å, det har vært en fantastisk tid!" og fortalte henne om opplevelsen med Bror Ryan og så videre. Jeg sa, "Kjære, jeg har noe å fortelle deg. La meg vise deg." Jeg rakte ned i lommen og sa, "Ser du dette?" Jeg sa, "Jeg har alltid ønsket å være en evangelist. Jeg har fått nok invitasjoner til at det vil vare hele året. Vil du bli med meg?"
Hun svarte, "Selvfølgelig."
Vi skyldte fortsatt rundt hundre dollar på den gamle Forden, og hadde gjeld og andre utgifter, men hun ønsket å bli med.
Vi dro for å fortelle det til moren hennes. "Mamma," sa hun, "gå i vei." Men moren sa, "Bill, nei." Hun sa, "Det er bare en gjeng med avskrap fra andre menigheter, det som andre menigheter har sparket ut."

text:
"Why," I said, "they're the happiest people in the world. They're not ashamed of their religion. They just scream, shout, just as free as the water runs." I said, "I like that."
She said, "It's just what other churches has kicked out." She said, "It's nothing but a bunch of trash."
And she… And I come to found out, what she called trash, is the cream of the crop. And I say that with respect. That's exactly right.
So, I said, "Well…" She said--I said, "Well, that's my wife."
And she said, "But, it's my daughter." She said, "She can go. If she goes, her mother will go to her grave broken hearted."
Then Hope started crying. She said… "There's where I made my fatal mistake, right there."
So, she said, "Well, if you want to, if you want to go, I will go with you."
text_no:
"De er de lykkeligste menneskene i verden," sa jeg. "De skammer seg ikke over sin religion. De roper og skriker, like fritt som vannet som renner." Jeg sa, "Jeg liker det."
Hun svarte, "Det er bare det andre menigheter har kastet ut. Det er ingenting annet enn søppel."
Jeg oppdaget senere at det hun kalte søppel, er det beste av det beste. Og jeg sier det med respekt. Det er helt riktig.
Så jeg sa, "Vel..." Hun sa - Jeg sa, "Vel, det er min kone."
Hun svarte, "Men det er min datter. Hvis hun går, vil moren hennes gå i graven med et knust hjerte."
Da begynte Hope å gråte. Hun sa... "Der gjorde jeg min fatale feil, akkurat der."
Så hun sa, "Vel, hvis du vil, hvis du vil gå, vil jeg gå med deg."

text:
And we went on, talked it over. Instead of listening to God, I listened to the woman. Now, she may … she might be sitting right here this afternoon for all I know. I don't see her, but she may be. She's a good woman, but she just didn't understand at that time.
Then, sorrow set in. Immediately… We had a… After that … a little while, another little fellow was born, a little girl called Sharon Rose.
The nineteen and thirty-seven flood come up. Sorrows begin to break in. Things went wrong at the church. My congregation begin to drop. Just get out of harmony with God one time… And friends, I will always regret it as long as I live. Right then, my church thought I was a fanatic. They still do. Not my church body at Jeffersonville. No, no. I mean the Baptist church that I belonged to.
text_no:
Vi fortsatte å snakke om det. I stedet for å lytte til Gud, lyttet jeg til kvinnen. Nå, hun kan... hun kan kanskje sitte her i dag for alt jeg vet. Jeg ser henne ikke, men det kan hende hun er her. Hun er en god kvinne, men hun forstod bare ikke den gangen.
Sorg satte inn umiddelbart. Etter en liten stund ble en annen liten krabat født, en liten jente ved navn Sharon Rose. Flommen i 1937 kom. Sorger begynte å bryte inn. Ting gikk galt i menigheten. Min menighet begynte å falle fra. Bare det å komme ut av harmoni med Gud én gang... Og venner, jeg vil alltid angre på det så lenge jeg lever. Akkurat da trodde menigheten min at jeg var en fanatiker. De gjør det fortsatt. Ikke menighetsmedlemmene mine i Jeffersonville. Nei, nei. Jeg mener den baptistmenigheten jeg tilhørte.

text:
At home, I was a black sheep because I didn't drink and things, and all the rest of them did. In society, I didn't dance, and didn't go to places, and didn't play cards and those things, so I was a black sheep there. In church, I was a fanatic. And I just found out that that back-wash was right where I belonged. I was one of them. Just exactly. They had something that was down in here, and deep was calling to the deep, and it was where God was trying to get me.
I ain't disregarding any other, any church, or nothing about it. Every person that's born of the Spirit of God, is a son of God. That's right.
text_no:
Hjemme var jeg et svart får fordi jeg ikke drakk som de andre. I samfunnet danset jeg ikke, gikk ikke på steder eller spilte kort, så der var jeg også et svart får. I menigheten ble jeg sett på som en fanatiker. Jeg oppdaget at dette utstøtte miljøet var akkurat der jeg hørte hjemme. Jeg var en av dem, helt og holdent. De hadde noe inni seg, og dypt ropte til dypt; det var hvor Gud forsøkte å føre meg.
Jeg ser ikke ned på noen menighet eller noe annet. Enhver person som er født av Guds Ånd, er en Sønn av Gud. Det er riktig.

text: But then, I remember when the flood came up, and my wife took sick. And I never forget that hour. My! The night the dike broke through down there, the levy, Brother Ryan, you was there. And I was working on the patrol. I thought I was a pretty good boatman. And I'm fixing to close the service. And I never forget that night, these few seconds … I want to try to put it to your hearts to let you know of what taken place at that time. My wife taken sick. And the dike broke through that night. I remember meeting Brother Ryan and them out there, and he was in my old boat, standing out facing the water, preaching to the people, going down the river.
text_no: Men så husker jeg da flommen kom, og min kone ble syk. Jeg vil aldri glemme det øyeblikket. Min Gud! Natten da diket brast der nede, bror Ryan, du var der. Jeg jobbet på patruljen og trodde jeg var en ganske god båtfører. Nå skal jeg avslutte møtet. Jeg vil aldri glemme den natten. Disse få sekundene … Jeg vil gjerne sette det i hjertene deres og la dere få vite hva som skjedde på den tiden. Min kone ble syk, og diket brast den natten. Jeg husker at jeg møtte Bror Ryan og de andre der ute. Han sto i min gamle båt, vendt mot vannet, og forkynte for folkene som drev nedover elven.

text: And then, I went to the hospital to get her, and the whole thing had washed away. And there was, out in the… Went out to make a rescue to a woman, they'd come, told me there was somebody out there on [unclear words] street, and a house was going in and the people were drowning. And I got in the boat, and pulled… Little old pull string motor, and I got out there, and got to where the woman was, and the house was just about to go over. A big two story house shaking back and forth. And I come down through the back of a alley like, where I had to get in. And I tied the boat, went and got a mother and some children and put them in the boat. Mother fainted. I got her in the boat, packing her. Put her in the boat, got back out. And when she come to, when we got her to shore, she begin to scream, "My baby! My baby!" And I thought she left a baby in that house up there.
text_no: Deretter dro jeg til sykehuset for å hente henne, og alt hadde vært vasket bort. Ute i gaten... Jeg dro ut for å redde en kvinne etter å ha blitt fortalt at noen på en gate holdt på å drukne i et hus som var i ferd med å bli skylt vekk. Jeg satte meg i båten med en liten motor og kom meg til stedet hvor kvinnen befant seg. Huset, et stort to-etasjes hus, vaklet faretruende fram og tilbake. Jeg manøvrerte båten gjennom en bakgate og fortøyde den. Jeg gikk inn i huset og fant en mor og noen barn, som jeg deretter fikk om bord i båten. Moren besvimte, men jeg bar henne ut og fikk henne trygt i båten. Da vi nådde land, våknet hun og begynte å skrike: "Min baby! Min baby!" Jeg trodde hun hadde etterlatt et barn i det huset der oppe.

text: Well, I tried to go back again. Come to find out, it was a baby she was… A little two year old, three year old child she had there with her, and she didn't know where it was at. But, I'd done got in the boat, and I went back to get the baby. And as I tied the boat to the post, like this, and got in, begin to look around, there was nothing in the house, the bottom give away. And here I was in the house. And I run, jumped through the door real quick, and fell into the water, and got the post like this, and pulled a string loose. Jumped up in the boat again, and the current had done got me out in the middle of the Ohio River, which is three or four times as wide as it is here, and then it was really wide. The whole city was swept over.
text_no: Jeg forsøkte igjen å dra tilbake. Det viste seg at hun hadde et lite barn med seg, et to eller tre år gammelt barn, og hun visste ikke hvor det var. Jeg hadde allerede gått i båten, men jeg gikk tilbake for å hente barnet. Da jeg bandt båten til en stolpe og begynte å lete rundt, oppdaget jeg at huset var tomt; bunnen hadde gitt etter. Der var jeg, inne i huset. Jeg løp raskt ut gjennom døren, falt i vannet, grep tak i stolpen og løsnet tauet. Jeg hoppet tilbake i båten, men strømmen hadde tatt meg med ut midt i Ohio-elven, som er tre eller fire ganger så bred som her, og da var den virkelig bred. Hele byen var oversvømt.

text: And the motor… Something had happened, I couldn't get it started. And that current was taking me out in towards the dam down there over the falls. It was just as hard as it could swirl me around. I was sitting out there pulling that string as hard as I could, and it wouldn't start. And I'd pull it again, the boat turning, the waves almost as high as this building. It was just like that. I had plenty of time to think about whether that was backwash or not, or trash. I thought, "Oh, my. This little [unclear words] over that [unclear words] and to that falls we'll go, and that's the end of it. I thought of wife, two babies. And I got to pulling the string like that, and I was going on out into the river. And I seen it wasn't going to start, and I started crying. I said, "God, be merciful to me. Don't let me die out here like this." I pulled the string like that, and pulled it again. It wouldn't start. And I choked it, and I had it flooded, and I was pulling again. I thought, "Oh, my!" Why, I couldn't … I didn't know what to do. And then, just about time I got in the current, it started.
text_no: Jeg kunne ikke få start på motoren. Strømmen dro meg mot demningen og fossen. Den virvlet båten rundt, og jeg drar i startsnoren så hardt som jeg kan, men motoren vil ikke starte. Båten snur, og bølgene er nesten like høye som bygningen. Jeg tenker på om det er tilbakeslag eller søppel, og jeg tenker: "Åh, nei. Denne lille båten vil gå over fossen, og det vil være slutten." Jeg tenker på min kone og to barn mens jeg desperat drar i snoren, men vi driver bare lenger ut i elva. Jeg ser at motoren ikke vil starte, og jeg begynner å gråte. Jeg sier: "Gud, vær barmhjertig. Ikke la meg dø her." Jeg drar i startsnoren igjen, men motoren er oversvømmet, og ingenting skjer. Akkurat når jeg er i strømmen, starter motoren endelig.

text:
I whirled around and got back. Come back way down towards New Albany, got in, went up to find my wife, the whole hospital was covered through [unclear words] with water. And I thought, "She's drowned and gone." She had Billy Paul back there, and Sharon. She was down with double pneumonia. So, I asked them, "What become of them?"
They said, "They'd got on a train and went out in a cattle car."
That sick mother, with a hundred and five fever, in a cattle car, and the sleet blowing just as hard as it could across… And then they said, "They went towards Charlestown." And I started out to hit Charlestown. Got my boat and got in there, there was about seven miles of water where a creek had backed up… Broke through this way, and the current coming just as hard as it could come. I tried hour after hour, and I couldn't even get that boat to even pierce that current. It would bring me right back around like this again. I tried and tried. And there I come to find out I was marooned out on a island to myself. And there I sat for days, thinking all about that backwash, kicked out of other churches.
text_no:
Jeg snudde raskt og dro tilbake. På vei ned mot New Albany, kom jeg fram og gikk for å finne min kone. Hele sykehuset var oversvømt. Jeg tenkte: "Hun har druknet og gått bort." Hun hadde Billy Paul og Sharon med seg. Hun lå med dobbeltsidig lungebetennelse. Jeg spurte dem: "Hva skjedde med dem?"
De sa: "De tok toget og dro i en kuvogn."
Den syke moren, med 40,5 i feber, i en kuvogn, og det blåste kraftig med sludd… Så sa de: "De dro mot Charlestown." Jeg satte kursen mot Charlestown. Jeg tok båten og kom dit, det var omtrent syv mil med vann der en bekk hadde flommet over… Vannet strømmet raskt. Jeg prøvde i timevis, men klarte ikke å få båten gjennom strømmen. Den førte meg rett tilbake igjen. Jeg prøvde og prøvde. Til slutt innså jeg at jeg var forlatt på en øde øy. Der satt jeg i flere dager, tenkende på alt som hadde skjedd, utstøtt fra andre menigheter.

text:
When I found her, after the waters went down, and I got to where she was way up at Columbus, Indiana, in a Baptist Hospital, a place, room like this, I went through there screaming at the top of my voice. I was about wild. And I seen her raise up her hand, and there my darling, she had fell off so much weight till she wouldn't weigh over a hundred pounds. That pneumonia had went into tuberculosis, and she was dying.
The intern come and got me, and take me back, said, "Just a minute. Aren't you a friend of Sam Adair?"
I said, "Sam Adair," that's a doctor in Jeffersonville, a buddy of mine.
text_no:
Da jeg fant henne etter at vannet hadde trukket seg tilbake, og jeg kom til Columbus i Indiana, på et baptistsykehus, et rom som dette, gikk jeg gjennom der og skrek så høyt jeg kunne. Jeg var nærmest desperat. Jeg så henne løfte hånden, og der lå min kjære, så avmagret at hun ikke veide mer enn femti kilo. Lungebetennelsen hadde utviklet seg til tuberkulose, og hun var døende.
Turnuslegen kom og hentet meg, og tok meg med tilbake. Han sa: "Et øyeblikk. Er ikke du en venn av Sam Adair?"
Jeg svarte: "Sam Adair," som er en lege i Jeffersonville og en kompis av meg.

text:
He said, "Well, look. We're going to send her to Sam," said, "the girl is going to die." Said, "Now, you're a minister, aren't you?"
I said, "Yes, sir."
Said, "Just don't go to her. Don't be excited."
I said, "All right."
And I straightened myself up and went down to see her. And I went in, she said, "Bill?"
And I looked, and her jaws sunk in, and those dark eyes way back. And I knelt down beside of her, and I started praying. We brought her home, her and the baby, we took her out the hospital. They done everything that could be done. Dr. Miller, here, Louisville, come to look at her. Said, "There isn't a thing can be done." And went on and on, until she laid just a few hours to live.
text_no:
Han sa: "Vel, hør. Vi skal sende henne til Sam," og sa, "jenta kommer til å dø." Han sa: "Nå, du er en forkynner, ikke sant?"
Jeg svarte: "Ja, det stemmer."
Han sa: "Bare ikke gå til henne. Ikke bli oppskaket."
Jeg sa: "Greit."
Jeg rettet meg opp og gikk ned for å se henne. Da jeg kom inn, sa hun: "Bill?"
Jeg så på henne, og kinnene hennes var innsunkne, og de mørke øynene lå dypt i hodet. Jeg knelte ned ved siden av henne og begynte å be. Vi tok henne og babyen hjem, ut av sykehuset. De hadde gjort alt som kunne gjøres. Dr. Miller fra Louisville kom for å se på henne og sa: "Det er ingenting som kan gjøres." Tiden gikk, og hun hadde bare noen få timer igjen å leve.

text:
And I was out on patrol when I heard them call me. And I turned on, come down the road as hard as I could. They said, "She's dying." Said, "Calling for Reverend Branham to come to the hospital. Wife's dying."
And I went into the hospital, I'll never forget it as long as I live. Rushed up the steps, went over to where she was laying there, looked at her, and she had already turned over on her side. Dr. Adair coming down the hall, bless his heart. We're neighbors now, and always been buddies. He was coming down the hall, he seen me coming, tears running down his cheeks. He darted in sideways. And I went in, asked, said, "What about it, Doc?"
We fished together, hunt together, lived together. He said, "Billy, she's probably gone by now."
I said, "Doc, let me hold your hand. Let's go in together."
text_no:
Jeg var ute på patrulje da jeg hørte dem rope på meg. Jeg snudde og kjørte så raskt jeg kunne nedover veien. De sa: "Hun er døende. Hun roper etter Pastor Branham for å komme til sykehuset. Kona dør." 
Jeg gikk inn på sykehuset, og jeg vil aldri glemme det så lenge jeg lever. Jeg løp opp trappene og bort til der hun lå. Hun hadde allerede snudd seg over på siden. Dr. Adair kom gående nedover gangen med tårer rennende ned kinnene. Han skyndte seg inn i rommet. Jeg gikk inn og spurte: "Hva med det, doktor?"
Vi hadde fisket, jaktet og bodd sammen. Han sa: "Billy, hun er sannsynligvis gått bort nå."
Jeg sa: "Doktor, la meg holde hånden Din. La oss gå inn sammen."

text:
He said, "Billy, I can't go in there." He said, "As many pies and things Hope's cooked for me and things." Said, "As good as she's been, like my sister." Said, "I can't go in, Bill." And he was breaking his own heart.
And I said, "Doc, I'm going in."
Said, "No, you sit here, Bill, just a little while, and we will let the undertaker come and get her."
And I said, "I'm going in, doctor."
He said, "You can't do it."
And I said, "Yes I can."
text_no:
Han sa: "Billy, jeg kan ikke gå inn der." Han sa: "Så mange paier og andre ting Hope har laget til meg." Sa: "Så god som hun har vært, som en søster for meg." Sa: "Jeg kan ikke gå inn, Bill." Og han var på vei til å bryte sitt eget hjerte.
Jeg sa: "Doktor, jeg går inn."
Han sa: "Nei, du sitter her, Bill, bare en liten stund, så lar vi begravelsesagenten komme og hente henne."
Jeg sa: "Jeg går inn, doktor."
Han sa: "Du kan ikke gjøre det."
Jeg sa: "Jo, det kan jeg."

text:
And he tried to pull me back, and I just went on. Walked down the hall, opened up the door and went in, and there she was laying like that, this sheet up over her. I pulled the sheet down. I looked at her laying there. I put my hand on her head, felt real sticky, and I said, "Sweetheart, can you hear me?" I shook her again. I said, "Do you hear me, honey?"
And if I live to be a hundred years old, I'll never forget those great, big, dark, angel eyes opened up. True, lovely woman, she looked up, twenty-two years old. Looked me right in the face. She said, "Oh, Bill." I knelt down and started crying. She put her arm over on me and begin to pat me. She said, "Why'd you call me back?"
text_no:
Han prøvde å dra meg tilbake, men jeg fortsatte. Jeg gikk nedover gangen, åpnet døren og gikk inn. Der lå hun med et laken over seg. Jeg trakk lakenet ned og så på henne. Jeg la hånden på hodet hennes; det føltes klissete. Jeg sa: "Kjære, kan du høre meg?" Jeg ristet henne igjen og sa: "Hører du meg, kjære?"
Hvis jeg lever til jeg blir hundre år, vil jeg aldri glemme hvordan de store, mørke, engleaktige øynene hennes åpnet seg. En virkelig vakker kvinne, bare tjue-to år gammel, så rett på meg. Hun sa: "Å, Bill." Jeg knelte ned og begynte å gråte. Hun la armen over meg og begynte å klappe meg forsiktig. Hun sa: "Hvorfor kalte du meg tilbake?"

text:
Just then, the nurse run in. Said, "Reverend Branham, you can't stay in there."
I said, "Just a minute, nurse." We knew her real well.
My wife called her over. She said, "Juanita," said, "I hope when you get married you have a husband like mine." Said, "He has been so good to me," and she had her arm around me.
And I said, "What was you talking about, honey?"
She said, "Bill, I was being taken home." The nurse left the room. And, she said, "I had been taken Home, and some Angel's like was going down." Said, "It was just so peaceful, a great tropic, " and said, "the big birds was flying from tree to tree." She said, "Now, don't think I'm beside myself."
And I said, "Yes."
What it was, her eyes were just opened to see paradise just… And she said, "You know why I'm going, don't you, Bill?" And that's what hurt.
I said, "I believe so, honey."
text_no:
Da løp sykepleieren inn og sa: "Reverend Branham, De kan ikke bli her inne."
Jeg svarte: "Et øyeblikk, sykepleier." Vi kjente henne godt.
Min kone kalte henne over og sa: "Juanita, jeg håper at når du gifter deg, får du en ektemann som min." Hun fortsatte: "Han har vært så god mot meg," mens hun holdt armen sin rundt meg.
Jeg spurte: "Hva var det du snakket om, kjære?"
Hun svarte: "Bill, jeg ble hentet hjem." Sykepleieren forlot rommet. Hun fortsatte: "Jeg ble hentet Hjem, og noen Engler var på vei ned." Hun beskrev det som en fredfull, tropisk scene med store fugler som fløy fra tre til tre. Hun la til: "Nå, ikke tro at jeg har mistet forstanden."
Jeg svarte: "Ja."
Øynene hennes ble bare åpnet for å se paradiset… Hun fortsatte: "Du vet hvorfor jeg drar, ikke sant, Bill?" Og det var det som gjorde vondt.
Jeg sa: "Jeg tror det, kjære."

text:
She said, "I hope I didn't influence you when I was crying that day, when her mother said that these people were back-wash."
I said, "No."
She said, "Bill, it's the most glorious thing in the world to die with the baptism of the Holy Ghost." She said, "I don't mind." Said, "I hate to leave you." Said, "But take care of Billy Paul." That's my boy sitting right there. Said, "Take care of him, and raise him a Christian." And said, "Then, you also… And Sharon, the little girl." And said, "Don't stay single." Said, "I want to ask you some things to promise me." Said, "Remember that time you wanted to buy that rifle in Louisville, and didn't have enough money to make a down payment? Two dollars?"
And I said, "Yeah."
text_no:
Hun sa, "Jeg håper jeg ikke påvirket deg da jeg gråt den dagen, da moren hennes sa at disse menneskene var avskum." Jeg svarte, "Nei."
Hun fortsatte, "Bill, det mest herlige i verden er å dø med Den Hellige Ånds dåp. Jeg har det bra med det," sa hun. "Jeg misliker å forlate deg, men ta vare på Billy Paul." (Det er min sønn som sitter der.) Hun sa, "Pass på ham, og oppdra ham som en kristen. Og så, du også... Og Sharon, den lille jenta." Hun sa, "Ikke bli værende alene. Jeg vil be deg om å love meg noen ting." Hun fortsatte, "Husker du den gangen du ville kjøpe det geværet i Louisville, men ikke hadde nok penger til å betale depositumet på to dollar?"
Jeg svarte, "Ja."

text:
She said, "After I'm gone, " she said, "go home, and look up under the folding bed on that newspaper. I was saving nickels to get enough money," to make the down payment on that rifle for me. She knowed I wanted it so bad.
You'll never know how I felt when I went home and seen about a dollar and seventy-five cents laying there. She'd saved it for months trying to get enough money to make that payment.
Said, "Will you promise me you will get that rifle?"
Said, "Yeah."
And she said, "Then, I don't want you to stay single." Said, "You get some good, Christian girl, with the baptism of the Holy Ghost that will raise the children right." Said, "Will you? I want you to meet me there at the gate."
Said, "All right, honey. But, I won't promise to get married again."
text_no:
Hun sa: "Etter at jeg er borte, gå hjem og se under den sammenleggbare sengen på avisen. Jeg sparte 5-øringer for å få nok penger til å betale forskuddet på det geværet. Hun visste at jeg ønsket det så sterkt.
Du aner ikke hvordan jeg følte meg da jeg dro hjem og så omtrent en dollar og syttifem cent liggende der. Hun hadde spart i måneder for å få nok penger til å betale forskuddet.
Hun sa: "Lover du å få tak i det geværet?"
Jeg svarte: "Ja."
Hun sa: "Jeg vil ikke at du skal forbli ugift. Finn en god, kristen jente med dåpen i Den Hellige Ånd som vil oppdra barna riktig. Vil du? Jeg vil møte deg ved porten."
Jeg sa: "Greit, kjære. Men jeg kan ikke love å gifte meg igjen."

text: She said, "Please promise me." Said, "I don't want my children pulled from post to post like that." And said, "Promise that you will never let down no more. That you will always preach this wonderful, glorious Gospel, and the baptism of the Holy Ghost." She said, "Bill, there's not one worry in the world that I have right now." She said, "I'm just as … she was just as willing to die as the water flows down that river. Said, "Just hate to leave you and the children." but, she said, "I'm going back." Said, "I have no desire to stay."
text_no: Hun sa: "Vennligst lov meg." Hun sa videre: "Jeg vil ikke at barna mine skal bli dratt med fra sted til sted slik." Og hun sa: "Lov at du aldri vil gi opp mer. At du alltid vil forkynne dette vidunderlige, herlige Evangeliet og dåpen i Den Hellige Ånd." Hun sa: "Bill, jeg har ikke én bekymring i verden akkurat nå." Hun sa: "Jeg er like villig til å dø som vannet flyter nedover den elven." Hun sa: "Jeg hater bare å forlate deg og barna," men la til: "Jeg drar tilbake." Hun sa: "Jeg har ikke noe ønske om å bli."

text: I said, "Honey, on that morning, you stand over on the east side of the gate. Somewhere, somewhere in the world, if I'm living, I will be preaching this Gospel until the time that I meet you. And if I sleep before then…" We don't believe in death. There's no Scripture in the Bible says a Christian dies. No, sir, they're not dead. And so, I said, "If I'm sleeping, I'll be by your side there at the grave." I said, "But, if I'm not, I'll be somewhere in the world preaching the Gospel. And I'll get the kids together, or you get them, and you stand on the east side of the gate. When you see Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and the rest of them coming up, I'll be there."
text_no: Jeg sa, "Kjære, den morgenen står du på østsiden av porten. Et eller annet sted i verden, hvis jeg lever, vil jeg forkynne dette Evangeliet helt til jeg møter deg. Og hvis jeg sover før det..." Vi tror ikke på døden. Det er ingen Skrift i Bibelen som sier at en kristen dør. Nei, de er ikke døde. Så jeg sa, "Hvis jeg sover, vil jeg være ved din side ved graven." Jeg sa, "Men hvis jeg ikke gjør det, vil jeg være et sted i verden og forkynne Evangeliet. Så får jeg barna sammen, eller du får dem, og du står på østsiden av porten. Når du ser Abraham, Isak og Jakob, og de andre komme opp, vil jeg være der."

text:
And she put her arms around me, and I kissed her goodbye. That was it. The Angels come packed her away. I took off home. Just as I got home, not knowing it, here come someone running and said, "Brother Branham?"
"Yeah."
Said, "You're baby's dying, also."
"Baby dying?"
"Yeah."
Little, fat healthy thing. I remember her mother used to put the little three corners on her, set her out in the yard, and I'd blow my horn when I was coming around. She was just big enough to jump and go, "Goo-goo, goo-goo, goo." Just sweet and plump. How I loved her.
I said, "My baby's not going?"
"Yep."
text_no:
Hun holdt rundt meg, og jeg kysset henne farvel. Det var det. Engelene kom og hentet henne. Jeg dro hjem. Da jeg kom hjem, uten å vite det, kom noen løpende og sa: "Bror Branham?"
"Ja," svarte jeg.
"Din baby er også døende," sa de.
"Babyen døende?"
"Ja."
Den lille, lubne, sunne skapningen. Jeg husker hvordan moren pleide å sette trekantede hatter på henne og plassere henne i hagen. Når jeg tutet med hornet på bilen på vei hjem, var hun akkurat stor nok til å hoppe og si, "Goo-goo, goo-goo." Så søt og lubben. Hvor høyt jeg elsket henne.
Jeg sa, "Min baby kommer ikke til å dø?"
"Jo," svarte de.

text:
I run to the hospital real quick. Sam was standing there. Said, "You can't go in, Bill." Said, "She's developed tubercular meningitis, and she's dying now."
I said, "Where's Billy Paul?'
Said, "We got him away." Said, "You can't go in, now." Said, "You will pick up that germ, take it back to Billy."
I said, "Surely, doctor."
I waited till he turned his back and I went in anyhow. And I went in, they had her in an isolating place. Not a very good hospital. Flies was all in her little eyes. And I went down there, and looked at the poor little thing. And I shook her. Her little legs was fat, and moving back and forth, little spasm like. And when she opened her little eyes and looked at me… She was blue-eyed. And those little blue eyes, she had suffered so hard till they were crossed. And when she looked at me, I said, "Sherry, do you know your daddy, honey?" Her little lips begin to quiver, and she was trying to reach for me, and was dying.
text_no:
Jeg løp raskt til sykehuset. Sam sto der og sa: "Du kan ikke gå inn, Bill. Hun har fått tuberkuløs meningitt og hun er døende nå."
Jeg spurte: "Hvor er Billy Paul?"
Han svarte: "Vi har fått ham bort." Han la til: "Du kan ikke gå inn nå. Du vil plukke opp bakterien og ta den med tilbake til Billy."
Jeg sa: "Selvfølgelig, doktor."
Jeg ventet til han snudde ryggen til, så gikk jeg inn likevel. Hun lå i et isolasjonsrom. Sykehuset var ikke spesielt bra; det var fluer i hennes små øyne. Jeg gikk ned dit og så på den stakkars lille jenta. Jeg ristet forsiktig i henne. De små beina hennes var fyldige og beveget seg rykkvis, som små spasmer. Da hun åpnet de blå øynene sine og så på meg... Hun hadde blå øyne. Hun hadde lidd så mye at de var blitt skjeløyde. Jeg spurte: "Sherry, kjenner du pappa, vennen min?" De små leppene hennes begynte å skjelve. Hun prøvde å rekke ut etter meg, men hun var døende.

text:
I knelt down, and I said, "O God, please, don't let my baby die. I'm sorry that I listened to what somebody else said. Take me, and let my baby live. I'm the one that sinned. I'm the one that did wrong." I said, "Let my baby live, God. Don't take her. I love her so much."
And while I was praying, I looked. Looked like a black sheet come folding down. I knowed. I knowed that was it.
Just a few minutes, the nurse come in. Said, "Reverend, you can't stand in here."
I said, "Just go on out."
Seen the Angel of God come, take the little thing home. I walked over and put my hand on her little head. I said, "Sweetheart, God bless your little heart." I said, "You're going to be a little angel directly, in the arms of mother. She's laying there in the morgue, now."
text_no:
Jeg knelte ned og ba: "Å Gud, ikke la barnet mitt dø. Jeg er lei for at jeg lyttet til hva noen andre sa. Ta meg i stedet, og la barnet mitt leve. Det er jeg som har syndet. Det er jeg som gjorde galt." Jeg sa: "La barnet mitt leve, Gud. Ikke ta henne. Jeg elsker henne så høyt."
Mens jeg ba, så jeg en svart duk som foldet seg ned. Jeg visste at det var slutten.
Et par minutter senere kom sykepleieren inn og sa: "Pastor, du kan ikke bli her inne."
Jeg svarte: "Bare gå ut."
Jeg så Guds Engel komme og ta barnet hjem. Jeg gikk bort og la hånden på hennes lille hode. Jeg sa: "Kjære, Gud velsigne ditt lille hjerte. Du blir en liten engel og vil snart være i armene til moren din. Hun ligger i bårehuset nå."

text:
I said, "God, I've done wrong, but someday, if You forgive me, I'll make it all right to You." I said, "You gave her to me, You're taking her away. Blessed be the name of the Lord." I said, "I love You, Lord, with all my heart." I felt her little flesh quiver. She was gone.
I couldn't hold myself together. My bones wouldn't hold together, looked like. I was dying. I taken her, put her in her mother's arms, take her up there on the hill, dug a hole. I was standing there, and Brother Smith, the Methodist church, my buddy, preached the funeral. I heard him reach over and get those clods and say, "Ashes to ashes, and dust to dust, earth to earth."
Just then, whispering down through those pine trees, come a wind, seem like it was singing:
There's a land beyond the river,
That they call the sweet forever,
We only reach that shore by faith's decree;
One by one we gain the portals,
There to dwell with the immortals,
Someday, they will ring those golden bells
for you and me.
text_no:
Jeg sa: "Gud, jeg har gjort feil, men en dag, hvis Du tilgir meg, skal jeg gjøre det godt igjen for Deg." Jeg sa: "Du ga henne til meg, Du tar henne bort. Velsignet være Herrens navn." Jeg sa: "Jeg elsker Deg, Herre, av hele mitt hjerte." Jeg følte hennes lille kropp skjelve. Hun var borte.
Jeg klarte ikke å holde meg sammen. Kroppen min føltes som om den skulle falle fra hverandre. Jeg tok henne og la henne i morens armer, tok henne opp på høyden og gravde en grav. Jeg sto der, og Bror Smith fra metodistmenigheten, min venn, holdt begravelsen. Jeg hørte ham ta grus og si: "Aske til aske, jord til jord, støv til støv."
Akkurat da, hørte jeg en hvisken gjennom furutrærne, en vind som virket som om den sang:
Det er et land bortenfor elven,
Som de kaller det søte evige,
Vi når bare den bredden ved troens dekret;
En etter en når vi portalene,
Der for å bo med de udødelige,
En dag, vil de ringe de gylne bjellene
for deg og meg.

text:
Here recently, my boy, he was just a little bitty fellow, we was taking a flower over to his mother's grave. He had his hat in his hand, and a little flower holding it on Easter morning. He started snubbing, and crying … Billy Paul, the one that helps me here in the service. I put my arm around him. Walked up, and he set the little flower down just as it was breaking day. I said, "Now stand up, honey." I said, "Mother and sister, their body lays there, but way across the sea yonder there is an empty tomb. Some glorious day, by His death and resurrection this will be empty, and we will be with them again. So, don't worry, honey."
I couldn't stand it. I tried to work. I tried … I could see my wife going, but my baby? I just couldn't get over it.
text_no:
For ikke lenge siden tok min lille gutt og jeg en blomst til morens grav. Han holdt hatten sin i hånden og en liten blomst på påskemorgen. Han begynte å snufse og gråte. Billy Paul, som hjelper meg her i møtene, var med. Jeg la armen rundt ham og vi gikk bort til graven. Han satte blomsten ned akkurat idet dagen gryte. Jeg sa: "Nå, stå opp, kjære." Jeg sa: "Mor og søster ligger der, men langt borte på den andre siden er en tom grav. En herlig dag, på grunn av Hans død og oppstandelse, vil denne graven være tom, og vi vil være sammen med dem igjen. Så ikke vær bekymret, kjære."
Jeg klarte det ikke. Jeg prøvde å jobbe, men jeg kunne ikke komme over det. Jeg klarte å akseptere at min kone var gått bort, men mitt lille barn? Det var for vanskelig.

text:
I remember one evening started to come from work. I picked up the mail on the side of the house, and I looked. It said, "Miss Sharon Rose Branham." Her little Christmas saving, eighty cents. I went in, I was trying to bathe in our little old two rooms there, and one of them, I never had no fire in the other side. Frost come up through the floor, and I knelt down there by my little old stove, and my cot, and I was praying. I said, "O God, why did You take her?"
And while laying there praying, sobbing away in the night, I must have fell asleep. And I dreamed that I seen, going walking along … I've spent a lot of time, about twenty years, in cattle, in the West. I was going walking on. I had on my hat, big hat, and I was kicking my spurs along, just going on along like that, whistling that song, "The wheel on the wagon's broken. A sign on the ranch For Sale.'" And I looked, and there was an old prairie schooner there, and the wheel was broke down. And I looked, and there stood a beautiful, young girl standing there. She said, "Hello, daddy."
And I said, "Who are you?"
text_no:
Jeg husker en kveld da jeg kom hjem fra jobb. Jeg plukket opp posten ved siden av huset og så på en konvolutt. Den var adressert til "Frøken Sharon Rose Branham" og inneholdt hennes lille juleinnsparing på åtti cent. Jeg gikk inn, klar til å bade i våre små to rom, hvorav det ene ikke hadde ildsted. Frost kom opp gjennom gulvet, og jeg knelte ned ved min lille komfyr og feltseng, og jeg ba. Jeg sa, "Å Gud, hvorfor tok Du henne?"
Mens jeg lå der og ba, gråtende gjennom natten, må jeg ha sovnet. Jeg drømte at jeg gikk en tur ... Jeg har tilbrakt mye tid, omtrent tjue år, med kvegdrift i Vesten. Jeg gikk videre, iført hatten min, en stor hatt, og sparka med spora mens jeg plystra den sangen: "Hjulet på vogna er ødelagt. Et skilt på ranchen: Til salgs." Jeg så en gammel prærievogn, og hjulet var ødelagt. En vakker, ung jente stod der og sa, "Hei, pappa."
Jeg svarte, "Hvem er du?"

text:
And she said, "I'm your little Sharon." She said, "Mother's waiting for you up at your new home."
I said, "new home?" I said, "We've never had a new home, honey."
She said, "You got one up here, daddy."
And I started up, and I hear them singing that song, "I see the lights of the city so bright." And I got up, and there she was standing there, looking at me. She put her arms around me, and greeted me, as she always did. She said, "Won't you sit down?"
text_no:
Hun sa: "Jeg er din lille Sharon." Hun fortsatte: "Mor venter på deg i ditt nye hjem."
Jeg svarte: "Nytt hjem?" og la til: "Vi har aldri hatt et nytt hjem, kjære."
Hun smilte og sa: "Du har fått et her oppe, pappa."
Da jeg begynte å gå oppover, hørte jeg dem synge sangen "Jeg ser byens lys så klare." Da jeg kom frem, stod hun der og så på meg. Hun omfavnet meg og hilste på meg som hun alltid gjorde. Hun ba meg om å sette meg ned.

text:
And I looked, and there was a Morris chair. I started looking at that Morris chair, looked back at her, and she said, "I know what you're thinking about."
Down here one time, we had just one chair. And the chair only cost fifteen dollars, I was going to buy down here. And I made a two dollar payment on it, and was paying a dollar a week, you know, when you get to a place where you can't make ends meet. You all know what I'm talking about. It's no disgrace to be poor. I just couldn't make the ends meet, and I missed two or three payments, and they told me they was going to come get it. We just couldn't make the payments. And one day, when I come in, I never forget it. She'd baked me a cherry pie, and everything, and she said, "Come in." And I went in the front room, and my chair was gone. When I'd work hard all day, and preach half the night, and then come in and sit in that chair, cause I liked to. And they come took it from me.
text_no:
Jeg så en Morris-stol og begynte å studere den. Så kikket jeg tilbake på henne, og hun sa: "Jeg vet hva du tenker på."
En gang her nede hadde vi bare én stol. Stolen kostet bare femten dollar, og jeg hadde tenkt å kjøpe den her. Jeg hadde betalt to dollar i første avdrag og betalte en dollar i uken. Dere vet hvordan det er når man knapt får endene til å møtes. Det er ingen skam å være fattig. Jeg klarte bare ikke å få endene til å møtes, og jeg gikk glipp av to eller tre avdrag. De sa de skulle komme og hente den. Vi klarte bare ikke å betale for den.
En dag, da jeg kom hjem, husker jeg spesielt godt. Hun hadde bakt en kirsebærpai til meg og sa: "Kom inn." Jeg gikk inn i stua, og stolen min var borte. Etter å ha jobbet hardt hele dagen og forkynt halve natten, likte jeg å komme hjem og sette meg i den stolen. Men de kom og tok den fra meg.

text:
And she said, "Won't you sit down?" And the chair looked like it, only much bigger. And she said, "You remember that one down on earth?"
I said, "Yes."
She said, "Bill, they'll never take that one. That one's already paid for. It's yours. Sit down."
Excuse me, people. And some glorious day, some day, I'm going to preach my last sermon. I'm going to pray for the last person I will ever have to. But there's a chair sitting across the river, I want to sit down awhile.
Someone said to me not long ago, said, "Brother Branham, you're at home. You're going all night, and all day, and every day, and everything," said, "when do you ever get any rest?"
I said, "When I cross the River. I got a chair over there, I'm going to sit down and rest a little while."
text_no:
Hun sa, "Vil du ikke sette deg?" Stolen så ut som den, bare mye større. Hun fortsatte, "Husker du den på jorden?"
Jeg svarte, "Ja."
Hun sa, "Bill, de kommer aldri til å ta den. Den er allerede betalt. Den er din. Sett deg."
Unnskyld meg, folkens. En herlig dag, en dag, skal jeg preke min siste preken. Jeg vil be for den siste personen jeg noensinne må be for. Men det finnes en stol på den andre siden av elven hvor jeg ønsker å sette meg litt.
Noen sa til meg nylig, "Bror Branham, du er alltid hjemme. Du går hele natten, og hele dagen, hver dag og alt," sa de, "når får du noen gang hvile?"
Jeg svarte, "Når jeg krysser elven. Jeg har en stol der borte, og jeg skal sette meg ned og hvile litt."

text:
Let's bow our heads.
Lord, forgive me, Lord, for being a baby, but today I see them old ways, the scars and things, as I remember. God grant that the people, if there's any here, Lord, that's kind of undecided about what they're going to do hereafter, may they reach out, Lord, and touch Your hand. I believe at the other side, You have my beloved wife, my baby, my little Sharon. I thank You for restoring to me, Lord, all that I lost, and more. I love You. It's totally with all my heart, I want to serve You as long as I live. Don't make any difference what they call, or what they say, I want to serve You.
text_no:
La oss bøye våre hoder.
Herre, tilgi meg, Herre, for å være svak, men i dag ser jeg tilbake på de gamle veiene, arrene og tingene jeg husker. Gud, gi at menneskene, hvis det er noen her, Herre, som er usikre på hva de skal gjøre fremover, må de rekke ut, Herre, og ta Din hånd. Jeg tror at på den andre siden har Du min elskede kone, min baby, min lille Sharon. Jeg takker Deg for å gi meg tilbake, Herre, alt jeg mistet, og mer til. Jeg elsker Deg. Helt av hele mitt hjerte ønsker jeg å tjene Deg så lenge jeg lever. Det spiller ingen rolle hva de kaller meg, eller hva de sier, jeg vil tjene Deg.

text:
Dear God, there may be some poor, little, old, Kentucky brother, or sister sitting here this afternoon, that doesn't know You. I pray, God, if there is, that You will forgive them right now. Grant it, Lord. May they come to this. When that great time of rest comes, and the labors are all over, that we will sit down together in the kingdom of God. Hear the prayer of Your servant.
While we have our heads bowed just a moment. Are you here without God this afternoon? If you are, would you raise up your hand, say, "Brother Branham, I want to meet you over there. I want to share the fellowship together in God's Kingdom with you. Will you remember me in prayer?" Will you raise your hand up if you will? Say, "Remember me." Is there anyone in the building? God bless you, honey. God bless you, you, you.
text_no:
Kjære Gud, det kan være en fattig, liten, eldre Kentucky-bror eller søster som sitter her i ettermiddag og som ikke kjenner Deg. Jeg ber, Gud, at dersom det er noen, tilgi dem nå. La det skje, Herre. Må de komme til Deg. Når den store hviletiden kommer, og alt arbeid er over, må vi da sitte sammen i Guds rike. Hør bønnen fra Din tjener.
Mens vi har hodene bøyd et øyeblikk. Er du her uten Gud denne ettermiddagen? Hvis du er, vil du rekke opp hånden din og si: "Bror Branham, jeg vil møte deg der. Jeg vil dele fellesskapet i Guds rike med deg. Vil du huske meg i bønn?" Vil du rekke opp hånden din? Si: "Husk meg." Er det noen i bygningen? Gud velsigne deg, kjære. Gud velsigne dere, dere, dere.

text:
If God will hear my prayers, to open the eyes of the blind, and make the deaf to hear, and the cripples to walk, won't He hear if you seek Him after His righteousness?
Is there any here that hasn't got this wonderful baptism of the Holy Spirit? Never been born-again?
You say, "I belong to church, Brother Branham." Well, that won't work, sister, brother. It's all right to live here, but wait till you come down tasting death, then you will know. If you haven't the Holy Spirit, would you raise up your hand? Say, "Pray for me." Let every head be bowed, now. God bless you, lady. You, and you, and you, and you, and you.
Would you give us a little chord on the piano? I'm going to ask just a short season, now, while we remain just quiet as we can, those who are seeking God, will you come up here and stand at the altar. I want to shake your hand, put my hands on you, pray with you. Will you come, now? All right.
God bless you, sister.
text_no:
Hvis Gud vil høre mine bønner, åpne blindes øyne, få døve til å høre og lamme til å gå, vil Han ikke også høre deg hvis du søker Ham etter Hans rettferdighet?
Er det noen her som ennå ikke har mottatt den vidunderlige dåpen i Den Hellige Ånd? Aldri blitt født på ny?
Du sier, "Jeg tilhører menigheten, Bror Branham." Vel, det holder ikke, søster, bror. Det er greit nok her i livet, men når døden nærmer seg, vil du forstå. Hvis du ikke har Den Hellige Ånd, vil du rekke opp hånden? Si, "Be for meg." La oss nå bøye hvert hode. Gud velsigne deg, dame. Deg, og deg, og deg, og deg, og deg.
Kan du gi oss en liten akkord på pianoet? Jeg vil be om en kort stund med stillhet nå, mens vi alle forblir så rolige vi kan. De som søker Gud, kom opp her og stå ved alteret. Jeg vil gjerne håndhilse på deg, legge hendene på deg, be sammen med deg. Vil du komme nå? Greit.
Gud velsigne deg, søster.
